Article.ArticleTitle: Comprehensive performance of iron-loaded biochar adsorption of U(VI) at an industrial scale based on life cycle assessment and economic analysis.

Abstract.AbstractText: Amid China's pursuit of its "dual-carbon" goals, ensuring uranium resource security and managing radioactive wastewater are major challenges for sustainable nuclear energy development. This study evaluates the environmental and economic feasibility of iron-based magnetic biochar (Fe@biochar) as a recyclable adsorbent for U(VI) removal at the industrial scale. Life cycle assessment (LCA) and life cycle cost (LCC) analyses were applied to upscale laboratory data and assess four modification pathways under seven representative industrial scenarios. Results showed that industrial production doubles biochar yield and significantly enhances U(VI) adsorption. LCA results indicated that marine ecotoxicity and human non-carcinogenic toxicity were the dominant impacts, mainly driven by reagent and energy inputs, while the impregnation-pyrolysis pathway achieved the greatest offset of ionizing radiation due to its superior U(VI) reduction. LCC revealed that dry ball milling and ferrous sulfate impregnation were most cost-effective, with production costs of $29.1 and $35 per kg and low external costs ($3.9 and $5.8 per kg). Sensitivity analysis showed that replacing FeCl3 with Fe2(SO4)3 increased environmental burdens by 50 %. A comprehensive environmental index (CEI) integrating LCA and LCC indicated that FeCl3 impregnation-pyrolysis achieved a CEI of -19.6, suggesting a net environmental benefit. This work establishes an integrated LCA-LCC framework for scaling Fe@biochar production, demonstrating its feasibility for U(VI) wastewater treatment and providing a reference for sustainable industrialization of magnetic biochar.
